overview: This domain refers to the essential principles, technical safeguards, and governance mechanisms required to ensure responsible and secure AI adoption in higher education. It covers data protection, privacy, cybersecurity, access control, infrastructure security, and ethical risk management when using AI systems, both internal and external. As AI increasingly interacts with sensitive academic, personal, and institutional data, universities face growing demands to establish clear rules, technical controls, and awareness programs that mitigate risks and align AI use with legal and ethical standards, including GDPR or national regulations. Without comprehensive security and privacy frameworks, AI adoption may expose institutions to legal liabilities, data breaches, and loss of trust among students and staff. This domain reflects the university’s capacity to implement secure AI environments, protect personal and sensitive data, promote user accountability, and build AI literacy that includes awareness of privacy and security risks.

instructions.maturity_scoring: In order to estimate the current maturity level of particular university in the Security and privacy AI DTP area, there is a list of 7 scored questions/items with answers related to current progress in the questioned items on 0–2 scale (0-Not in place, 1-Partially in place, 2-Fully in place) provided in the table below. General rules for scoring items are described in chapter 2.

items.item: AI-specific internal policies and guidelines on privacy and security

score_anchors.0: No AI- specific policies or guidelines.
score_anchors.1: Drafted or partial policies exist, but not institution-wide or not enforced.
score_anchors.2: Approved, institution-wide AI privacy and security policies, regularly reviewed and enforced

items.evidence_examples: Approved policy documents, review logs, communication to staff/students
items.owners: Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) / Data Protection Officer (DPO)

items.item: Regulation of input of personal/sensitive data into external AI tools

score_anchors.1: Informal or partial restrictions; not monitored.
score_anchors.2: Formal prohibition or regulated process, with monitoring and enforcement.

items.evidence_examples: Policy statements, monitoring reports, incident logs

items.item: Technical measures to protect data and infrastructure when using AI

score_anchors.0: No technical safeguards specific to AI.
score_anchors.1: Some technical controls in place, but not comprehensiv e or regularly tested.
score_anchors.2: Comprehensi ve, regularly tested technical safeguards for AI systems.

items.evidence_examples: Security audit reports, system logs, penetration test results

items.item: Regular training on responsible AI use, including privacy and security

score_anchors.1: Occasional or voluntary training; limited coverage.
score_anchors.2: Regular, mandatory training for staff and students, with tracked participation.

items.evidence_examples: Training records, attendance logs, feedback surveys

items.item: Rules for transparency and accountability in AI use

score_anchors.1: Some rules exist, but not enforced or communicate d.
score_anchors.2: Clear, enforced rules for disclosure and accountability , with reporting mechanisms.

items.evidence_examples: Policy documents, disclosure forms, incident reports
items.owners: AI Ethics or Governance Committee / Legal Department

items.item: Use of internal AI tools/platforms compliant with privacy requirements

score_anchors.0: No preference or requirements for compliant tools.
score_anchors.1: Some compliant tools used, but external/non- compliant tools still prevalent.
score_anchors.2: Majority of AI tools are internal and complian, external use is controlled;

items.evidence_examples: Procurement records, tool inventories, compliance checklists

items.item: Integration of privacy and security in digital transformation strategy (GDPR, AI Act)

score_anchors.2: Fully integrated, with actions and monitoring.

items.evidence_examples: Strategy documents, action plans, compliance reports

score_ranges.interpretation: Initial awareness of AI privacy and security risks; basic alignment with national laws but no AI-specific policies; limited technical safeguards.
Institutional policies and technical measures in place; AI-specific rules defined; responsible AI practices promoted among staff and students.
Fully integrated security and privacy frameworks; robust internal AI tools; proactive user accountability; alignment with national and EU AI strategies.

build_action_plan.intro: Before approaching a higher level of maturity in the Security and privacy AI DTP area, the critical items from 3.9.2 need to be fully implemented. For each unmet critical item, the necessary action needs to be specified to be performed (see table of foundational actions below).

foundational_actions.action: Approve and communicate AI-specific privacy and security policies and guidelines.
Establish and enforce formal restrictions on input of personal/sensitive data into external AI tools.
Implement and regularly test technical safeguards for AI systems and data infrastructure.
Integrate privacy and security requirements into the digital transformation strategy, with GDPR/AI Act compliance.

Emerging.action: Raise awareness about AI-related privacy and security risks among staff and students.
Align existing institutional policies with national laws (e.g., GDPR).
Restrict use of external AI tools for sensitive data.
Introduce basic technical safeguards for AI use (e.g., encryption, access restrictions).
Introduce basic disclosure rules for AI use in academic outputs.
Emerging.owners: AI Ethics or Governance Committee / Faculty/Department Heads

Emerging.action: Prefer internal or EU-compliant AI tools where possible.
Mention privacy and security requirements in institutional digital strategies.

Established.action: Develop AI-specific internal policies (e.g., responsible use guidelines for theses and assignments).
Deploy internal AI tools with data protection measures.
Conduct regular training on AI ethics and security.
Promote transparency and accountability among users.
Established.owners: AI Ethics or Governance Committee / Faculty/Department Heads

Established.action: Establish and enforce formal procedures for restricting sensitive data input into external AI tools.
Regularly test technical safeguards and conduct security audits of AI systems.
Operationalize privacy and security requirements in digital transformation action plans.

Enhanced.action: Fully integrate AI security and privacy measures into the digital transformation strategy.
Foster AI literacy that includes privacy, security, and ethical dimensions.
Regularly review and update governance frameworks in line with evolving legislation (e.g., EU AI Act).
Enhanced.owners: CISO / Legal Department / AI Ethics or Governance Committee

Enhanced.action: Automate monitoring and auditing of sensitive data use in AI tools.
Integrate AI security into institutional cybersecurity and incident response systems.
Conduct periodic audits to ensure long-term compliance and accountability.
Enhanced.owners: AI Ethics or Governance Committee / Legal Department

kpis.intro: The following table presents key performance indicators (KPIs) for this area and target values required to achieve each maturity level. For each KPI, the corresponding owner should be specified.

items.name: rate of AI-specific privacy and security guidelines26 (share of faculties/departments or central units that have formally adopted and communicated the AI privacy & security guidelines)
deployed AI tools aligned with GDPR and security policies
staff/students trained in AI privacy and security (annual)

targets.Established: At least one AI security audit per year covering the main institutional AI systems
targets.Enhanced: Bi-annual full- scope AI security audit; ≥90 % of identified issues resolved within 60 days

items.name: systems that process institutional data and are operated on internal or EU-compliant secure platforms

owners_and_roles.AI Ethics or Governance Committee: Multidisciplinary committee ensuring ethical, transparent, and compliant AI deployment.
owners_and_roles.Chief Information Security Officer CISO: Senior officer responsible for cybersecurity and AI-related risk management.
owners_and_roles.Data protection officer - DPO: Institutional authority for GDPR and privacy compliance.
